predictions.text: TKO (UFC/WWE merger) will trade at 14x EBITDA despite Vince/Dana risks; media rights deals could double revenue
Ferrari stock overvalued at 55x PE ratio; brand moat + luxury margin stay strong but downside risk high
If forced to choose between TKO and Ferrari, would pick Ferrari despite higher upside in TKO

disagreements: Sam bullish on TKO monopoly + founder (Dana) track record; Shaan prefers Ferrari brand moat but questions stock valuation. Sam sees monopoly value, Shaan skeptical post-hype.
dynamics: First "Stock of Paloa" episode (new recurring format). Structured 20-minute pitches with slides (YouTube version). Sam uses Buffett framework (understandability, moat, management, margin of safety, financials); Shaan uses narrative/brand framework (Lindy effect, heritage, luxury margins). Both riff on risks (litigation, founder personality). Good collaborative tension; neither fully convinced by other's thesis.
frameworks: Warren Buffett stock criteria: understandability, economic moat, competent management, margin of safety, strong financials
From cigarette butts to brands: Buffett evolved from cheap stocks → brand companies (Coke, AmEx) after Charlie Munger influence
Monopoly valuation: truly defensible, 95%+ market share (UFC/WWE vs Bellator, AEW), worth premium multiple despite risks
Lindy effect: things that survive long will survive longer; Ferrari 70+ years = probable 50+ more years
Luxury margins: Ferrari $100K profit/unit vs Tesla $6.7K vs Ford 67 units to match Ferrari's profit per car
Selectivity as brand: waitlist, customer bans (Justin Bieber, pink car lawsuit $350K), bounty program for non-compliance
Founder-dependent risk: Dana White (UFC), Enzo Ferrari (founder; deceased), Vince McMahon (founder; legal issues)
Global expansion moats: UFC untapped in Africa/India/China, WWE dominates social (39M followers vs NFL's 29M)
learnings: Monopolies rare in business; TKO truly has 95%+ market share in combat sports (Bellator, AEW failed)
Founder personality matters: Dana's 'insanity' and anti-fragility (COVID→Fight Island) both strength and legal risk
Sports economics differ from leagues: fighters paid 15-17% of revenue (vs NFL/NBA's 50%+) creates lawsuit risk
Live sports unique moat: can't be disrupted by AI, must be watched live (ads unavoidable), consistent demand
Luxury brand defensibility: Heritage + exclusivity + high margins > volume. Ferrari refuses to grow production
Cultural aspirational power: Ferrari trademarked their red; no competitor can use it; top 15 most expensive cars ever sold mostly Ferrari
Brand discipline pays: Enzo refused $50K cars (mercenary) to stay luxury missionary; paid off with $72B valuation
Valuation risk: Ferrari at 55x PE is expensive; TKO at 14x EBITDA has debt risks; both require faith in story

banger_quotes.quote: You can't use AI to make two guys fight in a ring; UFC is AI-proof
Ferrari has been around 70 years; Lindy effect says it'll be around 70 more
Dana White is insane in a good way but there's liability in that
You don't choose Ferrari; Ferrari chooses you
Italians wouldn't do affiliate marketing; they're the luxury brand of ethnicities

Ferrari profit per unit: $100K+ (vs Tesla $6.7K, vs Ford 67 units needed)

Porsche/Mercedes/BMW/Volkswagen market cap: all less than Ferrari's $72B
S&P 500 10-year annual return: 10.5% (2.7x over decade)
Target stock pick multiple: 5x return in 10 years (vs index 2.7x)
notable: First "Stock of Paloa" episode (new recurring format where hosts pick stocks with 20-minute pitches). Sam backs TKO using Buffett framework; Shaan backs Ferrari using luxury brand/Lindy narrative. Both vote for Ferrari if forced to choose, despite Sam pitching TKO (risk vs safety trade-off). Neither convinced by stock valuations but impressed by business models. Episode highlights tension between founder-dependent moats (Dana, Enzo) and brand moats. Shaan refuses to discuss Italian accent after weak attempt.
